Feature detection finds distinctive, repeatable points in an image, corners and blobs that can be re-identified across frames. Below you can run an ORB feature detector live on your own camera feed:

```{=html}
<iframe src="pages/html/orb_camera.html" width="100%" height="775" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" allow="camera" style="display:block;border:0;"></iframe>
```

#### How it works

The demo above runs **ORB** (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF), a fast combination of a corner detector (**FAST**) and a binary descriptor (**BRIEF**). This feature detection method consists of four distinct steps, as shown below:

```{=html}
<img src="assets/images/orb-pipeline.svg" alt="ORB feature detection pipeline: FAST corner test, intensity-centroid orientation, rotated BRIEF descriptor, and scale pyramid" style="width:100%;max-width:940px;height:auto;display:block;margin:0.5rem auto;">
```

<br />

1. **FAST Corner test:** Look at a ring of 16 pixels around a candidate pixel $p$. Each ring pixel of intensity $I_x$ is classified against the centre intensity $I_p$ and a threshold $t$:
$$
S_x = \begin{cases}
\text{brighter} & \text{if } I_x \geq I_p + t \\
\text{similar}  & \text{if } I_p - t < I_x < I_p + t \\
\text{darker}   & \text{if } I_x \leq I_p - t
\end{cases}
$$
where $I_p$ is the candidate's intensity and $t$ a fixed threshold (the `FAST THRESHOLD` slider). $p$ is a corner if $\geq 9$ contiguous ring pixels are *all brighter* or *all darker*. Survivors are ranked by Harris corner score and the best $N$ (the `MAX FEATURES` slider) are kept.


```{=html}
<img src="assets/images/corner-test.svg" alt="Harris corner detection" style="width:100%;max-width:940px;height:auto;display:block;margin:0.5rem auto;transform:scale(0.75);">
```


2. **Corner Orientation:** Each corner is assigned an angle using the **intensity centroid** method, which points from the patch centre toward its brightest side (the orientation line in the demo's **Rich keypoints** view). Steering the descriptor by this angle $\theta$ is what makes ORB rotation-invariant.
3. **BRIEF Descriptor:** Take the $31\times31$ patch centred on the corner. A *fixed* set of 256 pixel pairs $(a_i,b_i)$ - read from a precomputed **lookup table**, already rotated to the corner's angle $\theta$ so the result is rotation-invariant - is sampled inside this patch. Each pair yields one bit by comparing intensity: $\tau_i = 1$ if $I(a_i) < I(b_i)$, else $0$. Concatenating all 256 gives a **256-bit (32-byte)** descriptor. Later, descriptors will be compared across frames to find correspondences - the same scene point seen in two images.
4. **Scale pyramid:** Steps 1–3 run on each level of a pyramid of $N$ (the `PYRAMID LEVELS` slider) progressively downscaled images (ratio = `PYRAMID SCALE FACTOR` slider). A feature is detected at whatever level it is sharpest, giving **scale invariance** - the fixed $31\times31$ patch covers more of the scene on coarser levels. The demo otherwise draws just a dot per keypoint; the descriptors feed the matching step next.
